summary: Gruber highlights David Ruddock's critique of Chrome OS as a stalled platform, where Android app compatibility has become a crutch rather than a solution. The Android apps running on Chrome OS are poorly adapted for laptop use, creating file handling nightmares and a fundamentally broken user experience. Gruber draws a historical parallel to the original 1984 Mac, which deliberately shipped without Apple II text mode compatibility, forcing developers to build proper GUI apps. He argues that Chrome OS's ability to run Android apps has had the opposite effect — stunting Google's motivation to develop truly native solutions. He also dismisses the long-proposed Android/Chrome merger as a bad idea that only non-product people like Sergey Brin promoted. The core argument is that platform crutches prevent platforms from reaching their potential.
key_points: Android apps on Chrome OS are a terrible user experience — designed for phones, containerized away from the OS, with broken file handling
The 1984 Mac succeeded partly because it shipped without backward-compatible text mode, forcing everyone to embrace the GUI paradigm
The Android-Chrome OS merger idea, championed by Sergey Brin since 2009, was never supported by product people like Andy Rubin because it's fundamentally wrongheaded
Platform crutches — backward-compatible escape hatches — can actively prevent a platform from maturing by removing the motivation to build proper native solutions
Even early Catalyst apps on Mac, which Mac users complain about, sound far better than Android apps running on Chrome OS
money_quotes: However much we Mac users are complaining about the early state of Catalyst apps, Android apps running on Chrome OS sound far, far worse.
Made no sense then, makes no sense now. Or, I should say, it makes no sense if you consider what Chrome and Android really are.
Brin was never a product person. It's telling, to me, that as far as I can tell Andy Rubin — who is most certainly a product person — never talked up this proposed merger.

I firmly believe that in an alternate universe where the 1984 Mac shipped with an Apple II-compatible text mode — even just a single app akin to MacOS's Terminal app today — the product would've failed.
Developers are lazy — a compliment! — and they would've been drawn to that crutch.

The existence of the 'Chrome OS can run Android apps' crutch has stunted Google's motivation to push the platform forward to solve the remaining tasks that the platform isn't suited for in ways that are truly native to Chrome.
key_insight: Backward-compatible crutches on new platforms don't bridge gaps — they remove the urgency to build proper native solutions, ultimately stunting the platform's evolution.
